title: Oak candle box with sliding lid
subtitle: A small jointed box in oak with a groove-fitted sliding lid
excerpt: A lidded candle box in English oak: four sides dadoed together and a base glued in, with a sliding lid in a top groove. The finished box is 280 mm long, 80 mm wide, and 70 mm deep. The project introduces the dado groove and the sliding lid groove cut with a tenon saw and chisel.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the dado groove box method; Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for the kitchen candle box form.

glossaryTerms.definition: Timber that has been dried to equilibrium moisture content. Dimensionally stable; suitable for jointed box work.
A slot cut across the grain of a board to receive the end of a mating piece. The main joint in this box.
A lid that rides in a groove cut along the inside of the top edges of the box sides, rather than being hinged or removable.
The direction of the wood fibres. Sawing with the grain gives a smooth face; sawing across the grain (crosscut) severs the fibres and leaves end-grain.

content.text: A candle box is a narrow, lidded storage box originally kept by the fireplace to hold household candles. The
joints at the corners and the
groove make this a good first joinery project: no mortise or tenon, only straight saw cuts and chisel paring.
English oak or ash is the traditional material; 12 mm board is the standard thickness for a box of this size.
oak: two long sides at 280 mm by 70 mm; two short ends at 80 mm by 70 mm; one base at 280 mm by 80 mm; one sliding lid at 270 mm by 80 mm by 6 mm (thinner stock or a planed-down piece). Confirm the
on all pieces runs along the length of the boards, not across. End-grain on a 12 mm face will be weak at the dado.

groove 12 mm wide and 6 mm deep on each end of the long side boards: this receives the short end pieces. Use the marking gauge to scribe both shoulders of each dado. Saw the shoulders with the tenon saw, keeping inside the line. Remove the waste with the 12 mm chisel, paring in from each face to the bottom of the groove. Test-fit the end piece in the dado; it should slide in under hand pressure without forcing.
Mark a groove 6 mm wide and 5 mm deep along the inside top edge of both long side boards, running the full length. This is the

track. Set the marking gauge to 6 mm from the top face and score the top shoulder; reset to 12 mm and score the bottom shoulder. Saw both shoulders and chisel out the waste. Test the lid in the groove; it should slide freely without rattle.

Dry-fit the four sides together before gluing. Apply PVA glue to the dado shoulders; push the end pieces into the dadoes and clamp across the width. Before the glue sets, check square by measuring both diagonals; they should be equal within 1 mm. Glue the base piece onto the bottom edges once the four sides are set.
Sand the outside faces with 320-grit along the

direction. Apply one coat of Danish oil to the outside surfaces; wipe off after ten minutes and allow to dry for four hours. Do not oil the sliding lid groove; oil swells the wood fibres and tightens the groove.

attrs.symptom: The lid sticks in the groove
attrs.cause: Groove too tight or swollen after gluing
attrs.fix: Plane or sand the lid edges by 0.5 mm on each face and re-test. Rub a candle stub along the groove track to lubricate without swelling the wood.

attrs.symptom: One corner is not square after clamping
attrs.cause: Clamping pressure applied diagonally rather than squarely
attrs.fix: Before the glue sets, push the high corner diagonally until both diagonals match. Adjust the clamp angle. A second clamp across the open diagonal is the standard correction.
